title: Shakshuka
tagline: Eggs poached in a spiced tomato-and-pepper sauce, desi-tuned with degi mirch and eaten with roti.
macroNotes: Estimated for the shakshuka itself (roti not included — each roti adds ~110 kcal and ~3 g protein).

For a leaner, higher-protein plate, use 4 eggs but pull one or two yolks, or stir 100 g crumbled paneer into the sauce before adding the eggs (adds ~18 g protein across both servings). A handful of soaked, squeezed soya granules simmered into the sauce also works and keeps it authentic-ish.
The oil is the main fat lever — 1 tbsp instead of 1.5 saves ~60 kcal per serving.

items: 1/2 to 1 tsp degi mirch (stands in for paprika + cayenne — colour plus mild heat)
Salt to taste + a pinch of sugar if the tomatoes are very tangy
Optional desi tweaks: 1/2 tsp kasuri methi (crushed) or a pinch of jeera at the start
Roti (2-3 pieces), or pita / crusty bread

steps.title: Soften the veg
steps.body: Heat 1.5 tbsp olive oil in a skillet over medium heat. Add the chopped onion and 1/2 red bell pepper and cook 5-7 minutes until softened. (If using kasuri methi or jeera for a desi touch, add the jeera now.)

steps.title: Garlic and spices
steps.body: Stir in the 2 minced garlic cloves, 1 tsp cumin and 1/2 to 1 tsp degi mirch. Cook about 1 minute until fragrant — don't let the spices catch.

steps.title: Cook down the tomatoes
steps.body: Add the 2-3 chopped fresh tomatoes and season with salt (and a pinch of sugar if they taste sharp). Simmer 10-15 minutes, stirring and mashing lightly with the spoon, until it breaks down into a thick-ish saucy base.

steps.title: Make wells and add eggs
steps.body: Use the back of a spoon to make 3-4 little wells in the sauce and crack one egg into each. Cover the pan with a lid.

steps.title: Poach
steps.body: Cook covered 5-7 minutes until the whites are just set but the yolks are still runny (or cook longer if you like them firm). Don't overcook — the residual heat keeps setting them.

steps.title: Garnish and serve
steps.body: Scatter over chopped coriander or parsley (and feta / chilli flakes if using). Serve straight from the pan with hot roti to scoop it up.

notes: Degi mirch is a great one-jar swap for paprika + cayenne — go up to 1.5 tsp if you like it hotter, or add a pinch of regular chilli powder for extra heat.
Fresh tomatoes are actually more authentic than canned here; no need to peel them for shakshuka, just chop fine and cook them down until saucy.
It eats like a spicy tomato-egg curry with roti — add a little kasuri methi or jeera to lean it further towards desi.
source: Adapted from my own cooking notes.
